metadata.working_title: Benchmarking deep learning variant callers on bacterial Oxford Nanopore sequencing data

abstract_sketch.problem: Variant calling is foundational for bacterial genomics (outbreak tracking, AMR prediction, phylogenetics). Historically, short-read Illumina has been the gold standard due to base-calling accuracy.
abstract_sketch.gap: Existing ONT variant-caller benchmarks focus on human genomes using older pores; no comprehensive bacterial assessment covers the R10.4.1 pore with fast/hac/sup basecalling and duplex reads, and deep-learning callers are trained on human DNA only.
abstract_sketch.approach: Benchmark seven ONT variant callers and Snippy (Illumina) across 14 Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacterial species using ONT R10.4.1 data basecalled in fast, hac, and sup modes (simplex + duplex) and paired deep Illumina sequencing. Evaluate with pseudo-real variant truthsets generated by projecting donor-genome variants (ANI ~99.5%) onto gold-standard assemblies.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Clair3 and DeepVariant reach SNP F1 of 99.99% and indel F1 up to 99.61% on sup data, surpassing Illumina medians of 99.45% (SNP) and 95.76% (indel). 10x ONT sup simplex depth matches full-depth Illumina. Homopolymer-driven indel bias is largely eliminated with sup basecalling.
abstract_sketch.impact: Deep-learning ONT variant calling is a credible replacement for Illumina in bacterial clinical and public-health genomics, enabling accurate calling at lower depth with modest compute.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Variant calling underpins outbreak tracking, AMR prediction, phylogenetics.
Illumina has been the mainstay for bacterial variant calling because of high base accuracy.
ONT offers near-real-time, portable sequencing valuable for outbreak response.
R10.4.1 pore + Dorado basecaller (fast/hac/sup) plus duplex reads dramatically improved ONT accuracy.
Previous ONT variant-caller benchmarks used human genomes and older pores.
Deep-learning callers are trained on human DNA, raising generalisability questions for bacteria (k-mer and modification differences).
This work: first comprehensive bacterial benchmark on R10.4.1 with 14 species; introduces pseudo-real truthset from donor genomes.

sections.title: Related Work
content_bullets: Prior ONT variant-calling benchmarks on human data: PrecisionFDA, GIAB-based studies.
Bacterial Illumina variant benchmarks (Bush et al., Olson et al. GIAB).
Deep-learning variant callers: Clair3, DeepVariant, Medaka, NanoCaller.
Traditional callers: BCFtools, FreeBayes, Longshot.
Truthset construction strategies: simulation, real donor genomes, pseudo-real approach.

sections.title: Methods
content_bullets: Sampling: 14 bacterial species, both Gram-positive and Gram-negative; GC content 30-66%.
Sequencing: ONT MinION Mk1b / GridION, R10.4.1 FLO-MIN114, Dorado v0.5.0 v4.3.0 models (fast/hac/sup), duplex for hac/sup; Illumina NextSeq 500/2000, 150bp PE.
Truthset: donor genome with ANI closest to 99.5%, bounded 98.40-99.80%; intersect minimap2 + mummer variant calls; decompose with BCFtools; exclude indels >50bp; apply to sample reference.
Callers: BCFtools 1.19, Clair3 1.0.5, DeepVariant 1.6.0, FreeBayes 1.3.7, Longshot 0.4.5, Medaka 1.11.3, NanoCaller 3.4.1; Snippy for Illumina baseline.
Alignment: minimap2 v2.26 --cs --MD -aLx map-ont; min depth 2 / base quality 10 consistent across callers.
Assessment: vcfdist 2.3.3 with --credit-threshold 1.0; precision, recall, F1 per variant-quality threshold.

subsections.title: Read-level accuracy across basecalling modes
content_bullets: Duplex sup median identity 99.93% (Q32); duplex hac 99.79% (Q27); simplex sup 99.26% (Q21); simplex hac 98.31% (Q18); simplex fast 94.09% (Q12).

subsections.title: Clair3 and DeepVariant deliver the highest F1 across SNPs and indels
content_bullets: SNP F1 99.99% (Clair3, DeepVariant on sup).
Indel F1: Clair3 99.53% sup simplex, 99.20% sup duplex; DeepVariant 99.61% sup simplex, 99.22% sup duplex.
Illumina medians: SNP 99.45%, indel 95.76%.
Fast model is an order of magnitude worse than hac/sup; SNP-only Longshot.

subsections.title: Sources of missed and false calls
content_bullets: Illumina recall is hurt by variant-dense regions (bimodal density distribution peaks near 20/100bp).
Masking repetitive regions raises Illumina F1 from 99.3% to 99.7%; Clair3 only 0.003% increase.
Clair3 sup FP indels: 5/8 homopolymer-adjacent; DeepVariant sup 8/11.
BCFtools retains persistent homopolymer bias across models.

subsections.title: Depth required for Illumina-parity calls
content_bullets: 10x ONT sup simplex (Clair3 / DeepVariant) matches or exceeds full-depth Illumina F1 on SNPs and indels.
At 5x, BCFtools matches Illumina SNP F1 on duplex sup; SNP precision for Clair3/DeepVariant duplex exceeds Illumina at 5x.

subsections.title: Compute requirements
content_bullets: DeepVariant median 5.7 s/Mbp and 8 GB memory (~38 min for a 4 Mbp genome at 100x).
FreeBayes max 597 s/Mbp (~2.75 days).
Clair3 median 0.86 s/Mbp, 1.6 GB memory (<6 min for a 4 Mbp, 100x genome).
Sup basecalling on GPU: median 0.77 s/Mbp (~5 min for 4 Mbp at 100x).

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Deep-learning callers outperform traditional methods and Illumina on bacterial data despite being trained on human DNA.
Homopolymer indel bias largely resolved by sup basecalling combined with deep-learning callers.
Low-depth calling (10x) is clinically useful; 25x recommended for deployment.
Compute-cheap Clair3 is practical for laptops and field settings.
Limitations: small variants only, single ANI point (~99.5%), mixed Illumina instruments (NextSeq 500 + 2000).
Implications for public-health genomics: portable, low-depth, accurate bacterial variant calling is feasible.
